title: General Company FAQ
text:
## Summary

Quick answers about Bougie Bams: what it is, where it operates, its newsletter, social channels, and wholesale inquiries.

title: General Company FAQ
text:
## Details

What is Bougie Bams? A luxury, intimate mahjong community offering curated mahjong mats, sets, and accessories plus styled live events. Founded by Patsy Miller.

Where is Bougie Bams located? Texas — events run in the north Austin metro area (Leander and Cedar Park area venues).

Is there a newsletter? Yes — the Bougie Circle. Subscribers get access to new collections, upcoming events, limited edition drops, and Patsy's picks. New email subscribers receive a 15% discount offer.

Where can I follow Bougie Bams? Instagram and Pinterest, both at @bougiebams.

Do you offer wholesale? Wholesale inquiries can be submitted through the contact form on bougiebams.com or by emailing patsy@bougiebams.com.

Do I need to know how to play mahjong? No. Events and the community welcome complete beginners through seasoned players.

title: General Company FAQ
text:
## Examples

- A visitor asking about a discount can be pointed to the Bougie Circle newsletter signup for 15% off.
- A boutique interested in carrying products should be routed to the wholesale contact option.

title: The Bougie Bams Story
text:
## Summary

Bougie Bams is a luxury, intimate mahjong community founded by Patsy Miller. It combines a curated web store of premium mahjong mats, sets, and accessories with beautifully styled live events in the north Austin, Texas area.

title: The Bougie Bams Story
text:
## Details

Bougie Bams was born from a love of mahjong and a belief that every gathering deserves to be memorable — stunning, warm, and nothing short of luxurious. The brand rejects plain folding tables and mismatched tiles in favor of intentionally styled tables where every detail, from the linens to the lighting, is chosen with care.

Founder and CEO Patsy Miller describes Bougie Bams as an extension of who she is: a colorful, slightly over-the-top Texan who believes life is better when people gather around a beautiful table.

The business has three connected parts: a web store of curated mahjong mats, sets, and accessories; live ticketed mahjong socials at local venues; and a welcoming community that spans complete beginners to seasoned players.

title: The Bougie Bams Story
text:
## Examples

- A customer asking who is behind Bougie Bams can be told about Patsy Miller and the Texas-born, luxury-community positioning.
- An assistant describing the brand should emphasize experience and community, not just products.

title: The Bougie Bams Story
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Describing Bougie Bams as only a store — events and community are core to the brand.
- Assuming events are for experienced players only; all levels are welcome.

title: Our Values and Standards
text:
## Summary

Bougie Bams stands on three product values — premium materials, artisan craftsmanship, and modern aesthetic — plus a community value: every level of player deserves a seat at a beautiful table.

title: Our Values and Standards
text:
## Details

Premium materials: Bougie Bams sources high-grade acrylics and metals so each tile has the right weight, sound, and hand-feel.

Artisan craftsmanship: sets are hand-finished with attention to detail, from crisp engraving to flawless polishing.

Modern aesthetic: the brand honors the tradition of the game while reimagining its look for the modern, design-conscious player.

Community: the atmosphere at the table matters as much as the tiles in a player's hand. Events and products are designed so beginners and seasoned players feel equally welcome.

title: Our Values and Standards
text:
## Examples

- When a customer asks why Bougie Bams products cost more than mass-market sets, point to materials, hand-finishing, and design.
- When describing an event, mention that the styling standard applies to every table, not just featured ones.

title: Our Values and Standards
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Reducing the brand to aesthetics only; material quality and feel are equal pillars.
- Overpromising specifics not in the catalog — check current product listings before making claims.

Items may be returned within 14 days of delivery if they are unused and in their original packaging.

title: Return and Refund Policy
text:

- Condition: items must be unused and returned in their original packaging.
- How to start: email patsy@bougiebams.com with the order number and reason for return. Support will reply with return instructions.
- Refund: once the returned item is received and inspected, the refund is issued to the original payment method.

title: Learning to Play at Bougie Bams
text:
## Summary

Complete beginners are welcome at Bougie Bams events. First-timers learn at the table in a supportive setting, and celebrating a first mahjong win is a community tradition.

title: Learning to Play at Bougie Bams
text:
## Details

Bougie Bams positions itself for all levels — from picking up tiles for the very first time to seasoned play. Community photo albums regularly celebrate first mahjongs, showing that new players are not just tolerated but featured.

What a first-timer can expect: a styled table, patient table-mates, guidance through the flow of draws, discards, and calls, and zero pressure to be competitive. The knowledge base's mahjong teaching and fundamentals articles support instructors and hosts working with new players.

Dedicated class formats (structured beginner lessons, private instruction) are not yet documented; the assistant should direct people to the events page and newsletter for current learning opportunities rather than describe formats that are not confirmed.

title: Table Styling at Bougie Bams Events
text:
## Summary

Every Bougie Bams event table is intentionally styled. Guests can choose from a library of more than 40 premium mats, tile sets, and racks so each table looks and feels the way they want.

title: Table Styling at Bougie Bams Events
text:
## Details

The styling library pairs mats, tile sets, and racks into coordinated tablescapes. Linens, lighting, and accessories are chosen with the same intention — the atmosphere at the table is treated as equal in importance to the game itself.

At Style-Your-Own events, guests build their own table combination from the library, making the styling part of the entertainment.

For hosts and internal staff, the practical checklist per table of four: one mat sized to the venue table, one complete American mahjong set with jokers, four racks with pushers, and coordinated linens and accents.

- A venue with smaller cocktail tables calls for 31 inch mats rather than 33 inch.

title: Event Venues
text:
## Summary

Bougie Bams hosts events at intimate north Austin metro venues. Recent venues include Rabbit Hole (24 seats) and Cork2Glass Wine Bar at 601 East Whitestone Blvd in Cedar Park (28 seats).

title: Event Venues
text:
## Details

Venue profile: the brand favors warm, upscale-casual spaces — wine bars and similar venues — that can seat 24 to 28 guests at tables of four with room for styled tablescapes.

What a venue needs to work: enough four-top tables (or tables that seat four comfortably), lighting that flatters the tables, space for a raffle and welcome area, and drink service that matches the elevated social feel.

Partnership angle: venues gain a sold-out weeknight crowd; Bougie Bams provides all game equipment and styling. Venue inquiries can go to patsy@bougiebams.com.

title: Blind Pass
text:
## Summary

A last-pass Charleston move where a player forwards tiles without looking at them.

title: Blind Pass
text:
## Details

On the final pass of either Charleston, a player who lacks three unwanted tiles may take one or two incoming tiles unseen and pass three tiles on. Also called a steal. It is legal only on the first-left pass of the first Charleston and the last-right pass of the second.

title: Blind Pass
text:
## Examples

- A player short of throwaway tiles announces a blind pass on last right.
- An assistant can use this entry to answer terminology questions briefly.

title: Blind Pass
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Assuming a blind pass is legal on any pass — it is restricted to the final pass of each Charleston.

title: Blind Pass

title: Courtesy Pass
text:
## Summary

An optional exchange of zero to three tiles between opposite players after both Charlestons.

title: Courtesy Pass
text:
## Details

After the Charleston rounds end, players seated across from each other may trade up to three tiles. The player who wants to exchange fewer tiles sets the count. Jokers may not be passed.

title: Courtesy Pass
text:
## Examples

- Two opposite players agree to exchange two tiles each before play begins.
- An assistant can use this entry to answer terminology questions briefly.

title: Courtesy Pass
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Forgetting that the smaller request controls the exchange size.

title: Self-Pick
text:
## Summary

Winning by drawing the final tile yourself from the wall rather than claiming a discard.

title: Self-Pick
text:
## Details

A self-picked mahjong is drawn from the wall on the winner's own turn. In standard American scoring it doubles the payout from all three opponents, and it stacks with the jokerless bonus.

title: Self-Pick
text:
## Examples

- A player draws the last tile they need and declares mahjong without a discard.

text:

title: Can I Exchange A Joker From A Dead Hand?
text:
## Summary

Yes, if the joker sits in a valid exposure made before the hand went dead. No, if the joker is part of the invalid exposure that killed the hand, or if the dead player was attempting a concealed hand.

title: Can I Exchange A Joker From A Dead Hand?
text:
## Details

Valid exposures made before disqualification stay on top of the dead player's rack, and active players may redeem those jokers with the matching natural tile on their turn — the dead player must make the swap when asked. Jokers inside the errant exposure that caused the dead hand return to the rack and are forfeited. A dead concealed hand returns entirely to the rack, so none of its jokers can be swapped.

title: Can I Exchange A Joker From A Dead Hand?
text:
## Examples

- A dead player's earlier valid kong with a joker: an active player exchanges a natural tile for it normally.
- A joker in the invalid exposure that caused the dead hand: unavailable for the rest of the round.

title: Can I Exchange A Joker From A Dead Hand?
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Treating every joker on a dead player's rack as forfeited.
- Trying to redeem a joker from a dead concealed hand.

title: Can I Exchange A Joker From A Dead Hand?

title: Can I Take Back A Discard?
text:
## Summary

No — once the tile touches the table or you have fully named it, the discard is locked. The only exception is a tile that was only partially named and has not yet touched the table.

title: Can I Take Back A Discard?
text:
## Details

A discard becomes final at whichever comes first: the tile touching the tabletop, or the full name being spoken aloud. Saying "Five..." and stopping, with the tile still in hand, leaves room to retract. Saying "Five Bam," or letting the tile land, does not.

title: Can I Take Back A Discard?
text:
## Examples

- "Five Cr—" with the tile still in the air: retractable.
- Tile placed face up silently: locked the moment it touched the table.

title: Can I Take Back A Discard?
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Assuming a silent discard can be recalled because it was never named.
- Letting go of the tile while deciding — placement locks it.

title: What Happens If I Declare Mahjong In Error?
text:
## Summary

If you have exposed nothing, you may take the call back with no penalty. If you exposed only the claimed tile or one exposure, you may retract the win but that exposure stays. If you exposed more of your hand, your hand is dead.

title: What Happens If I Declare Mahjong In Error?
text:
## Details

The severity scales with what the table has seen. A verbal call alone is retractable. One committed exposure locks that exposure but keeps you alive. Revealing additional rack tiles disqualifies you: your turn ends without a discard, new exposures return to your rack, earlier valid exposures stay up with their jokers exchangeable, and you still pay the winner at the end. For authoritative rulings, consult the current official NMJL rulebook.

title: What Happens If I Declare Mahjong In Error?
text:
## Examples

- Call, realize, retract before showing tiles: play continues normally.
- Call and lay down your whole hand incorrectly: your hand is dead for the rest of the round.

title: What Happens If I Declare Mahjong In Error?
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Flipping the entire rack before verifying the hand against the card.
- Continuing to draw and discard after being declared dead.

title: What Is A Wall Game?
text:
## Summary

A wall game is a hand that ends with no winner: the wall is exhausted, the final discard is made, and no one has declared mahjong. In most social play no money changes hands and the deal simply rotates.

title: What Is A Wall Game?
text:
## Details

When the last wall tile is drawn and the final discard draws no mahjong call, the hand is over. Standard social practice treats it as a clean reset — shuffle and move the deal. Some groups roll a kitty to the next winner, and some tournament formats give a small bonus to players whose hands were still viable while dead hands receive nothing.

title: What Is A Wall Game?
text:
## Examples

- Four defensive players run the wall out; nobody pays, and the next dealer builds new walls.
- A league table awards viable hands a flat bonus after a wall game.

title: What Is A Wall Game?
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Paying out on a wall game when the group's standard is no exchange.
- Forgetting that forcing a wall game is a legitimate defensive outcome.

title: What Is A Wall Game?
text:

title: Blind Passes and the Courtesy Pass
text:
## Summary

A blind pass lets a player forward unseen tiles on the final pass of either Charleston. The courtesy pass is an optional 0-3 tile exchange across the table after both Charlestons, sized by the player who wants to pass fewer tiles. Jokers may never be passed.

title: Blind Passes and the Courtesy Pass
text:
## Details

Blind pass (steal): allowed only on the last pass of the first Charleston (first left) and the last pass of the second Charleston (last right). A player short of unwanted tiles may take one or two of the incoming tiles unseen, combine them with their own, and pass three tiles on.

Courtesy pass: after both Charleston rounds, players seated opposite each other may exchange zero to three tiles. The smaller request controls — if one player offers three and the other offers one, exactly one tile is exchanged.

Stopping the second Charleston: the first Charleston is mandatory; the second is optional and any player may stop it. Intent should be flagged during the first-left pass (asking the table to go slowly). Once a player picks up and looks at tiles from the second round's first pass, it is too late to stop it.

Jokers: jokers may never be passed in any Charleston pass or in the courtesy pass.

title: Blind Passes and the Courtesy Pass
text:
## Examples

- Holding only two tiles worth passing on a last-right pass, a player blind-takes one incoming tile to complete their three.
- One player wants a three-tile courtesy exchange, the other wants zero: no tiles are exchanged.

title: Blind Passes and the Courtesy Pass
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Blind passing on a middle pass — it is only legal on the final pass of each Charleston.
- Passing a joker during the Charleston, which is never allowed.

title: Blind Passes and the Courtesy Pass
text:

title: Incorrect Exposures and Wrong Tile Counts
text:
## Summary

An invalid exposure can be fixed only until the player discards or exchanges a joker; after that the hand is dead and jokers in the bad exposure are forfeited. A hand with the wrong number of tiles is also dead. Consult the current official NMJL rulebook for authoritative rulings.

title: Incorrect Exposures and Wrong Tile Counts
text:
## Details

Incorrect exposures: an exposure is invalid when it does not match a legal combination — for example using a joker in a single or pair, exposing on a concealed-only hand, or displaying a pattern not on the card.

- Correction window: the player may fix the exposure during their turn, up until they discard or perform a joker exchange. After that, the exposure is locked.
- If caught later: the hand is declared dead. The incorrect portion returns to the sloped rack, and jokers inside the errant exposure cannot be redeemed by anyone. Valid exposures made earlier stay on the rack with exchangeable jokers.

Wrong tile count: an active hand must hold exactly 13 tiles (14 only momentarily when drawing or winning).

- Too many or too few tiles: the hand is dead. The player stops playing but still pays the winner.
- Three players wrong: the entire hand is voided and redealt.
- During the Charleston: a wrong count found before play begins triggers a full reshuffle and redeal — with one exception: if only the player left of the dealer is missing a tile, they may draw from the wall to correct it before the dealer's first discard.

Wall destruction: a player who prematurely throws in their hand and disturbs the wall pays each remaining intact hand the lowest value on the card.

title: Incorrect Exposures and Wrong Tile Counts
text:
## Examples

- A player exposes a pung but meant a kong, notices before discarding, and corrects it freely.
- A player found with 12 tiles mid-game is declared dead but remains liable to pay the winner.

title: Incorrect Exposures and Wrong Tile Counts
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Trying to fix an exposure after discarding — the correction window has closed.
- Redealing the whole game when only the player left of the dealer is short one tile.

title: Incorrect Exposures and Wrong Tile Counts
text:

title: Declaring Mahjong in Error
text:
## Summary

The consequences of a false "Mahjong!" call depend on how much the player has exposed: nothing exposed means a free retraction, a single committed exposure means play continues with that exposure locked, and exposing more of the hand means disqualification. Consult the current official NMJL rulebook for authoritative rulings.

title: Declaring Mahjong in Error
text:
## Details

No tiles exposed: the player may retract the declaration without penalty and play continues.

Only the claimed tile or one exposure placed: the player may retract the win declaration, but that exposure stays committed on the rack.

Hand exposed: if the player reveals additional tiles from the rack, the hand is declared dead. The turn ends immediately with no discard, the newly exposed tiles return to the rack, and valid exposures made before the error stay up — their jokers remain exchangeable. Play continues with the next player. If the error followed a claimed discard that another active player could use to win, that player may claim it and win.

A disqualified player stops drawing, discarding, and calling hands dead, but still pays the eventual winner.

Multi-player errors: if several players declare in error and one hand remains intact, the last player to err pays the intact hand double the value of the hand they attempted. A player who throws in their tiles prematurely and destroys the wall pays each intact hand the lowest value on the card.

title: Declaring Mahjong in Error
text:
## Examples

- A player says "Mahjong," realizes the hand is wrong before exposing, and takes it back — no penalty.
- A player exposes their full rack, is declared dead, and sits out the rest of the hand but still pays the winner.

title: Declaring Mahjong in Error
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Exposing the whole hand before double-checking against the card.
- Assuming a dead player's earlier valid exposures lose their jokers — they stay exchangeable.

title: Declaring Mahjong in Error

title: Naming and Retracting Discards
text:
## Summary

Discards must be named aloud and placed face up; a tile is locked once fully named or once it touches the table. A misnamed discard penalizes the caller who relies on the wrong name, not the discarder. A discard stays callable until the next player racks a drawn tile.

title: Naming and Retracting Discards
text:
## Details

Announcing: place the discard face up in the center and clearly name it — "Five Dot," "North," and so on.

When a discard locks: the tile cannot be taken back once it touches the table or has been fully named. A partially named tile ("Five...") that has not touched the table may still be retracted.

Misnaming: if a discarder says the wrong name, the risk falls on players who act on the spoken name. Calling the misnamed tile for an exposure kills the caller's hand; the misnaming discarder faces no penalty. The exception is winning: a mahjong claimed on a misnamed tile stands.

Live vs. dead discards: a discard remains live — available to call — until the next player draws and racks a tile. Once racked, the prior discard is dead forever. If the next player holds their drawn tile without racking it, the earlier discard stays open until they discard or exchange a joker.

title: Naming and Retracting Discards
text:
## Examples

- A player says "Three Bam" while throwing a Three Crak; an opponent calls it for an exposure of bams and their hand is dead.
- A player begins saying "Six—" and pulls the tile back before it lands: legal retraction.

title: Naming and Retracting Discards
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Calling a tile based on what was said rather than looking at the tile itself.
- Assuming a discard stays callable after the next player racks their draw.

title: Scoring and Payouts Overview
text:
## Summary

In American Mahjong, the winner collects a base value set by the current card, adjusted by how the win happened: the discarder of a winning tile pays double while the others pay single, a self-picked win is paid double by everyone, and a jokerless hand doubles the payout again.

title: Scoring and Payouts Overview
text:
## Details

Base value: every hand on the current card carries a printed value that reflects its difficulty. That value is the starting point for all payouts. This article describes the general structure only — consult the current official card for values.

Winning on a discard: when a player wins on another player's discard, the discarder pays the winner double the base value; the other two players each pay the single base value.

Winning by self-pick: when a player draws their own winning tile from the wall, all three opponents pay double the base value.

Jokerless bonus: completing a hand with no jokers doubles the payout from all players. This bonus does not apply to Singles and Pairs hands, which cannot use jokers anyway and carry higher printed values instead.

Stacking: a self-picked, jokerless win compounds both bonuses — each opponent pays four times the base value, the standard maximum multiplier.

Wall games: if the wall runs out with no winner, no money changes hands in most social play; the deal rotates and a new hand begins. Some groups use a kitty that rolls to the next winner, and some tournament formats award small bonuses to players whose hands are still viable.

title: Scoring and Payouts Overview
text:
## Examples

- A hand won on a discard: the discarder pays 2x the card value, the other two pay 1x each.
- A jokerless self-pick: all three opponents pay 4x the card value.

title: Scoring and Payouts Overview
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Applying the jokerless double to Singles and Pairs hands.
- Forgetting that dead-hand players still owe the winner at the end of the game.

title: Protecting Your Hand
text:
## Summary

Strong players guard information as carefully as tiles: they delay exposures until the hand is well developed, keep their rack arrangement unreadable, and avoid physical and verbal tells that reveal what they need.

title: Protecting Your Hand
text:
## Details

Delay exposures: every exposure narrows what your hand can be and surrenders flexibility. A useful habit is waiting until roughly seven or eight tiles of the target hand are in place before making a first call, unless the call is clearly decisive.

Manage your rack: experienced opponents read racks. Gaps between tile groups, flipped blocks, and left-to-right structure all leak information. Keep spacing uniform and place new exposures to the left of the rack so the hand's internal order stays hidden.

Control tells: sighing at a discard, hesitating before passing, or staring at one end of the rack telegraphs your position. Aim for the same tempo and demeanor whether a discard helps you or not.

Keep flowers early: flowers appear in many hands on a typical card, so passing them early in the Charleston is usually a gift to an opponent.

Have an exit plan: pair this discipline with a pivot hand that shares tiles with your primary target, and switch to pure defense when your hand dies rather than feeding live discards.

title: Protecting Your Hand
text:
## Examples

- A player waits to call a pung because the hand is only five tiles developed, keeping a concealed option alive.
- A player rearranges their rack to even spacing after an opponent glances at it repeatedly.

title: Protecting Your Hand
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Making a quick early exposure that reveals the hand family and forfeits the concealed bonus.
- Leaving visible gaps between groups on the rack, effectively publishing the hand's structure.

title: Mat Care Guide
text:
## Summary

Keep mats clean by spot-treating spills promptly with a soft, slightly damp cloth, letting the mat air dry flat, and storing it rolled rather than folded.

title: Mat Care Guide
text:
## Details

General care guidance (material-specific instructions pending confirmation of mat fabric composition):

- Spills: blot immediately with a dry cloth, then spot-clean with a cloth dampened with water and a drop of mild soap. Avoid soaking.
- Drying: air dry flat, away from direct sunlight and heat.
- Storage: roll the mat around a tube or loosely on itself; folding creates permanent creases.
- Avoid: machine washing, bleach, harsh solvents, and ironing unless the confirmed fabric care says otherwise.

Wine and food are common at mahjong tables, so recommend coasters and treating spills before they set.

title: Mat Care Guide
text:
## Examples

- A customer who spilled red wine should blot first, then spot-clean, and avoid rubbing the stain deeper.
- A customer storing mats between game nights should roll them and keep them out of hot cars.

title: Mat Care Guide
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Folding mats for storage, which creases the play surface.
- Machine washing before confirming the fabric allows it.

title: Choosing Racks and Pushers
text:
## Summary

A full table needs four racks, ideally with attached pushers. Choose racks that fit your tile size, feel stable on the table, and complement your mat and set aesthetically.

title: Choosing Racks and Pushers
text:
## Details

Function: racks hold each player's hand upright and hide it from opponents; the top shelf displays exposures. Pushers — the arms attached to many modern racks — square up and push out the wall cleanly during the deal.

What to check:

- Tile fit: oversized tiles need deeper rack channels. Confirm compatibility before mixing brands.
- Pushers: combined rack-and-pusher designs speed up the deal and are the standard at styled events.
- Stability: heavier racks stay put when players draw and rack tiles quickly.
- Style: rack color is a styling decision; at Bougie Bams events, racks are matched to the mat and tile palette as part of the tablescape.

text:

title: Choosing a Tile Set
text:
## Summary

When choosing a mahjong tile set, weigh four things: material and hand-feel, engraving crispness, whether it is configured for American mahjong (jokers and blanks included), and the aesthetic you want on your table.

title: Choosing a Tile Set
text:
## Details

Material and feel: high-grade acrylic tiles offer satisfying weight, a pleasant click, and durability. Bougie Bams curates sets built from premium acrylics and metals, hand-finished for consistent polish.

American mahjong readiness: American play requires jokers, so confirm any set includes joker tiles and ideally spare blanks. Standard American sets run 166 tiles.

Engraving: crisp, deeply engraved characters stay readable for years and are easier for beginners to distinguish across the table.

Aesthetics: tile color, engraving color, and case design set the tone of the table. Bougie Bams leans into modern, design-forward looks that pair with its mats.

Size matters too: larger tiles are easier to read and handle, but confirm they fit your racks.

title: Choosing a Tile Set
text:
## Examples

- A beginner buying a first set should prioritize readable engraving and a full American configuration with jokers.
- A customer matching a Pink Snake Bandana mat can choose a tile color that complements the mat design.

title: Choosing a Tile Set
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Buying a Chinese-style set without jokers for American play.
- Choosing on looks alone and ending up with lightweight tiles that feel flimsy.

title: Tile Care Guide
text:
## Summary

Clean acrylic tiles with a soft cloth and mild soapy water, dry them fully before storing, and keep them out of prolonged heat and direct sun to protect the finish.

title: Tile Care Guide
text:
## Details

- Routine cleaning: wipe tiles with a soft microfiber cloth. For grime, use lukewarm water with a drop of mild dish soap, then dry immediately.
- Avoid: alcohol, acetone, ammonia cleaners, and abrasive pads — these can cloud or scratch acrylic.
- Oils: hands transfer oils during play; a quick wipe-down after game night keeps engraving paint bright.
- Storage: store tiles dry, in their case, away from hot cars and windowsills. Heat can warp acrylic and fade engraving color.
- Travel: pad the case so tiles do not rattle face-to-face, which can chip edges over time.

title: Tile Care Guide
text:
## Examples

- After a long event, a host can wipe tiles down before racking them back into the case.
- A customer asking how to remove sunscreen residue should use mild soapy water, not solvent wipes.

title: Tile Care Guide
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Using disinfectant wipes with alcohol, which dulls the acrylic surface.
- Storing sets in a hot garage or car trunk in Texas summers.
